name: Environment-Behavior Fidelity Framework
consequences: Immediate: Participant arousal responses and sleep architecture vary 15–40% depending on environmental familiarity and perceived clinical context. Systemic: Choosing clinical-grade monitored bedrooms versus home-like suites directly affects parasomnia event frequency capture; clinical spaces trigger suppression of naturalistic confusional arousal morphology, reducing Aim 2 phenotyping specificity by estimated 20–30%. Strategic: Low fidelity produces publishable but context-limited characterizations; high fidelity increases ecological relevance but introduces variability that complicates inter-participant comparison and algorithm training.
options: Maintain strict residential character across all 8 active suites with visible monitoring equipment removed or hidden, fire-safety signage minimized, personal furnishings and lighting control, accepting 10–15% lower inter-suite standardization in environmental conditions

Deploy a hybrid model with 5 home-environment suites (full domestic feel, minimal visible sensors) and 3 semi-clinical suites (standard hospital-grade beds, visible safety rails, explicit EEG and EMG montages visible) allowing within-study comparison of environment effects on event phenotype

Standardize all suites to a clinical-residential aesthetic with visible but aesthetically refined sensor infrastructure, neutral décor, and controlled environmental parameters (temperature, humidity, light cycle), accepting that this optimizes reproducibility over ecological validity
review: Controls Ecological Validity vs. Experimental Standardization. Weakness: The options do not address how environmental choice interacts with participant expectancy bias—participants assigned to 'home-like' suites may perform differently because they believe monitoring is lighter, potentially confounding any comparison of true environment effect versus perceived monitoring intensity.

name: Recruitment Stratification and Episode-Yield Targeting
consequences: Immediate: Broad inclusion (self-reported ≥2 episodes/month across all NREM parasomnia types) recruits 25–30% of candidates but 20–25% produce zero captured events despite 10-week maximum stay, yielding unproductive admissions and wasteful per-diem costs (€5,600–€7,000 per participant). Systemic: Stratifying by episode frequency, bed-partner corroboration, and prior PSG evidence of arousal clustering front-loads recruitment rigor, reducing unproductive admissions to 10–12% and shifting €150K–€200K saved per-year into expanded enrollment or deeper phenotyping, enabling Aim 2 analysis on 60+ instead of 45 participants. Strategic: Strict stratification risks excluding rare or subsyndromal presentations, limiting generalizability; loose inclusion maximizes phenotypic diversity but sacrifices statistical power and publication timeline.
options: Retain the current criterion of clinical interview plus collateral history with screening PSG only if prior polysomnography is available; admit all candidates who self-report ≥2 monthly episodes and meet exclusion criteria, accepting the 20% unproductive-admission ceiling and triggering no protocol change unless ceiling is exceeded

Implement three-tier enrollment: Tier 1 (high yield) requires ≥4 weekly episodes plus bed-partner corroboration plus evidence of arousal clustering on prior PSG; Tier 2 (standard) requires ≥2 monthly episodes plus one additional criterion; Tier 3 (exploratory) single-criterion admissions capped at 15% of cohort, tracking outcomes separately to establish gradient

Adopt a dynamic threshold strategy beginning with loose inclusion and progressively tightening after month 9 based on observed unproductive-admission rate; if ≥20% of admitted participants produce zero events, shift recruitment emphasis toward candidates with weekly frequency and documented prior PSG arousals, publishing the enrollment-refinement process as a methods paper
review: Controls Participant-Yield Efficiency vs. Phenotypic Diversity. Weakness: None of the options explicitly account for referral-source bias—neurologists and sleep clinics may preferentially refer more severe or atypical cases, skewing the episode-capture distribution regardless of inclusion criteria; the options lack a mechanism to monitor and adjust for source-dependent selection.

name: Sensing Tiering and Annotation Load Optimization
consequences: Immediate: Full PSG on every night (16-channel scalp EEG, EOG, chin/leg EMG, respiratory) generates ~1.5 GB/night per suite; 8 suites × 30 nights/month = 360 GB/month. Systemic: This volume demands €80K–€120K annual storage and backup infrastructure, 2.5–3 FTE senior scorers at €60K–€75K salary each for dual-rating at AASM standard, and real-time annotation bottlenecks exceeding night-tech capacity, creating 2–4 week scoring delays that slow feedback loops and reduce event-escalation responsiveness. Strategic: Tiered acquisition (nightly low-burden headband + selective full PSG on first, mid, and end nights plus escalation nights) cuts annual data volume to ~90 GB, reduces scoring demand to ~1.2 FTE, and frees annotation budget for algorithm development; trade-off is that intra-stay morphology evolution for individual participants is sampled rather than continuous, potentially missing clustering or habituation patterns.
options: Deploy full 16-channel PSG every night across all participants and all 8 suites, partnering with University Hospital Bonn's existing polysomnography scoring service (cost ~€25K–€30K/year for institutional rate), accepting longer turnaround and storage constraints but ensuring complete morphological documentation

Implement strict tiered model: dry-EEG headband + contact-free mattress sensors nightly; full PSG only on admission night, one randomly selected mid-stay night, and discharge night; technician-flagged escalation nights capture recurrence; budget ~€15K/year for storage and ~1.2 FTE scorer, reallocating savings to event-triage algorithm development

Hybrid tiering with outcome-adapted allocation: admit participants with low prior event frequency (monthly or less) under full-PSG protocol to maximize capture; admit high-frequency sleepwalkers (weekly or more) under selective tiering; stratify analysis by protocol arm to account for sensing differences, adding methodological complexity but optimizing data fidelity for rarer phenotypes
review: Controls Physiological Resolution vs. Operational Scalability. Weakness: The options do not address inter-rater reliability validation across different sensing modalities—if some participants have nightly full PSG and others have selective PSG, the dual-rater agreement benchmark must account for differential data quality, but the options provide no explicit frame for this comparison, risking publication of unreliable sensitivity/specificity claims for the event-triage tool.

name: Stay Duration Strategy and Facility Throughput
consequences: Immediate: A 2–8 week baseline with optional 2-week extension (capped 10 weeks) yields on average 4–5 weeks per participant; 8 active suites rotating at 80% occupancy = ~12–14 admissions per year, reaching 36–42 participants over 3 years, falling short of the 50–70 target. Systemic: Shortening the baseline to 10–14 days increases annual throughput to 18–20 admissions, enabling 54–60 three-year enrollment and 25–30% higher event-capture volume per dollar spent on per-diem and infrastructure. Strategic: Shorter stays risk missing rare or infrequent episodes (the 20% unproductive-admission ceiling assumes adequate time); longer stays improve event yield but reduce capacity and leave facility operating below 8-suite utilization or force early expansion that Aim 1 explicitly defers.
options: Maintain the current 2–8 week baseline with extension capped at 10 weeks, targeting 50–70 enrollment by aggressive recruitment and accepting that some participants will occupy suites without generating events; use unproductive stays as a research opportunity to characterize suppression factors and inter-individual variability in environment-induced sleep inhibition

Standardize all stays to 14 days except where zero events occur by day 10, triggering a protocol decision point: discharge with participant re-contact to reschedule if circumstances change, or extend by 7 days only if PI adjudicates high clinical likelihood of near-term event based on history and PSG arousal architecture; expected outcome is 15–16 admissions per year and 55–60 total enrollment with ≤10% unproductive admissions

Implement time-varying stay allocation: admit high-frequency episodic patients (≥weekly) under a 10-day standard; admit lower-frequency patients (2–3 monthly) and exploratory RBD participants under a 3–4 week baseline, allocating longer stays to phenotypes with lower baseline event density; re-allocate facility capacity dynamically based on monthly event-capture metrics to maximize yield per bed-night, accepting complexity in scheduling and ethics reporting
review: Controls Enrollment Velocity vs. Event-Capture Yield. Weakness: The options do not quantify the interaction between stay duration and event-suppression habituation—if longer stays actually increase event frequency due to familiarity, or decrease it due to adaptation, the options provide no mechanism to measure and respond to this dynamic, risking that the duration strategy is set empirically without evidence and cannot be adjusted mid-trial.

name: Event-Triage Tool Scope and Validation Rigor
consequences: Immediate: Developing a semi-automated triage algorithm requires labeled training data from ≥100 captured events with dual-rater agreement as ground truth; underfunding this work (budget <€150K/3-year) yields a heuristic tool (rule-based event flagging) with sensitivity ~60–70% and specificity ~50–65%, useful for night-tech guidance but unpublishable as a standalone methods contribution. Systemic: Investing €250K–€350K over three years (2 postdocs + dedicated data engineer time) enables machine-learning–based approaches with reported sensitivity 75–85%, specificity 70–80%, and ablation studies showing which sensor modalities drive performance; this tier enables publication as a co-primary Aim 3 result and positions the research unit for follow-on funding on decision-support tools. Strategic: High-ambition triage validation (requiring prospective testing on held-out 20% of events with independent external rater agreement) delays publication by 6–12 months and risks never achieving publishable metrics if event yield is lower than projected or training data is too heterogeneous.
options: Develop a rule-based triage algorithm using expert-defined heuristics (e.g., rapid EEG slowing + mattress sensor displacement + infrared motion within 3-second window = probable parasomnia event); evaluate sensitivity and specificity against dual human raters on the full training dataset without held-out validation; publish as a feasibility tool with caveats, accepting that the tool is transparent but limited in generalizability

Conduct supervised machine-learning model development with systematic feature engineering from PSG and sensor modalities, cross-validated sensitivity/specificity reporting on an 80/20 train-test split, and external evaluation against a secondary cohort of 15–20 independent control participants run through the facility in parallel; budget €300K over three years and target publication of model architecture and performance metrics as a standalone Methods and Applications paper

Deploy an adaptive semi-automated system that begins with rule-based flagging in year one, gathers prospective event labels from dual-rater annotations, and transitions to iterative machine-learning refinement in year two with monthly model retraining; publish year-one results as preliminary tool development and year-three results as a prospective-validation study, accepting higher complexity in reporting but enabling stronger claims about generalizability
review: Controls Publication Velocity vs. Algorithmic Sophistication. Weakness: None of the options address how class imbalance in training data—the vast majority of sensor readings are non-event background—will be handled; if the algorithm is trained without explicit attention to imbalance, false-positive rates may be prohibitively high in real-time operation, undermining the tool's utility for technician triage regardless of sensitivity/specificity on the balanced test set.

name: Domestic Environment Preservation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reduced clinical signage and monotone décor decreases participant anxiety and suppresses behavioral inhibition. Systemic: 15–20% improvement in natural sleep architecture metrics and event frequency capture via naturalistic home-like surroundings versus sterile monitoring suites. Strategic: Elevated event yield enables recruitment targets and publication-grade phenotyping analysis, justifying the €750K renovation cost over generic clinical retrofit.
options: Furnish suites with individually selected residential décor (artwork, adjustable lighting, personal bedding options) while integrating sensors invisibly into existing furniture and wall panels, accepting higher setup time per participant.

Deploy mixed aesthetic across cohorts—half residential, half clinical—and systematically measure sleep-stage stability and subjective comfort to empirically optimize environment design for year-two expansion.

Maintain clinical-grade safety infrastructure (padded edges, alarm systems) but conceal it under residential finishes, with technician-accessible modularity hidden behind walls and closets, prioritizing perceptual domesticity.
review: Controls **Naturalistic Behavior Capture vs. Institutional Safety Overhead**. Weakness: The options do not address the labor cost of individually customizing suites—per-suite décor curation and technician time for furniture-embedded sensor integration could exceed the renovation budget contingency, undermining year-one pilot timeline.

name: Event-Yield Recruitment Calibration
consequences: Immediate: Tightening pre-admission screening (specialist interview, bed-partner collateral, prior PSG) filters out ambiguous cases and reduces 20% false-positive enrollments. Systemic: 70–80% event-capture success rate stabilizes operational predictability and reduces extended-stay extensions from 30% to <10% of participants, saving ~€180K in participant compensation over three years. Strategic: Predictable event yield unlocks reliable publication timelines and algorithm-training datasets, enabling month-18 go/no-go decision on year-three funding.
options: Implement graduated pre-admission screening with tiered confidence scores (high/medium/low parasomnia probability) and admit only high-confidence cases in year one, deferring medium-confidence referrals to year two after validation of facility safety and data quality.

Enroll unselected adults aged 18–65 reporting any sleep disruption alongside parasomnia cohort as expanded controls, accepting lower event-capture yield but broadening generalizability to sleep fragmentation phenotypes and increasing enrollment throughput.

Partner with University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic to conduct pre-admission polysomnography on all referrals before facility enrollment, adding 2–3 week screening delay but eliminating <2% false-positive admissions and enabling event-frequency stratification for cohort balancing.
review: Controls **Recruitment Speed vs. Event-Yield Certainty**. Weakness: High-confidence screening in option 1 may over-select for severe, clinically obvious cases, potentially missing subtle within-person variability and generalizability to mild-to-moderate parasomnia presentations—this biases the Aim 2 phenotyping dataset.

name: Sensor-to-Annotation Bottleneck Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Tiered sensing model (nightly low-burden dry EEG, enhanced nights full PSG, escalation nights post-event) generates 40–60% data volume versus continuous full-PSG, reducing annotation hours per participant-night from 4–6 to 2–3. Systemic: Annotator throughput increases from 20 to 35 participant-nights monthly with same technician team, preventing €400K+ year-two hiring expansion and accommodating protocol timeline. Strategic: Real-time technician annotation flags pathological events for same-night escalation PSG, enabling iterative capture refinement and semi-automated triage algorithm training on high-quality dual-scored examples.
options: Implement strict nightly schedule (enhanced nights on nights 1, 14, 28 regardless of events) with escalation PSG only after technician flags, accepting false-negative missed events on non-enhanced nights in exchange for predictable staff workload and cost control.

Deploy fully continuous PSG acquisition (all nights, all participants) with post-hoc automated event detection to pre-screen recordings before human annotation, reducing annotation load to 1–2 hours per night but requiring €120K year-one investment in signal-processing infrastructure.

Reserve enhanced and escalation PSG exclusively for participants in the top event-frequency quartile (≥4 events per week by self-report), allocating full-PSG resources only to high-yield cases and redirecting low-event participants to dry-EEG-only protocol after initial characterization.
review: Controls **Data Richness vs. Annotation Labor Scaling**. Weakness: Option 1's fixed schedule ignores participant-specific event clustering; a participant with 8 events in week 1 and none in weeks 2–3 wastes enhanced-night PSG capacity, while option 3's quartile-based allocation creates tier-dependent data quality that complicates inter-participant comparison for Aim 2 analysis.

name: Staffing Safety Margin and Incident Protocol
consequences: Immediate: Overnight technician coverage model (3 rotating staff per 8-suite capacity) enables one technician per 2.7 suites plus shared coverage for event response and inter-suite equipment troubleshooting, reducing response latency to <2 minutes for floor incidents. Systemic: Safe incident management (minor arousal-related falls, confused behavior during parasomnia episode) during night shift occurs with documented technician presence and no escalations to emergency services in >95% of incidents, protecting participant safety record and institutional liability. Strategic: Sustained safety performance over 12 months justifies expansion to 12 suites in year-two protocol amendment without proportional staffing increase, amortizing personnel costs and enabling 50–70 participant enrollment within budget.
options: Maintain 3-person rotating night shift indefinitely, capped at 8-suite operation, with formal incident-response training quarterly and post-incident debriefs, accepting staffing cost (~€180K annually) and operational ceiling rather than scale beyond 8 participants simultaneously.

Hire 4-person night team from start (€240K year-one cost increase) to enable immediate 12-suite expansion and 1 technician per 3-suite unit, reducing response latency further but frontloading personnel expense and committing to full operational scale before pilot validation.

Adopt tiered on-call model: 2 in-facility technicians cover 8 suites nightly; 1 additional on-call technician within 15 minutes (local residency requirement) responds to escalated incidents, adding €60K contingency cost but maintaining budget flexibility for year-one pilot before scaling commitment.
review: Controls **Safety Redundancy vs. Personnel Cost**. Weakness: Option 3's on-call model introduces dependency on single off-site technician availability and assumes <15-minute response suffices for behavioral emergencies; if on-call technician is unavailable (illness, traffic), incident response lapses to 2-person team, creating unmodeled safety risk during high-event nights.

name: De-Identified Data Governance and Repository Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Restricting public data deposit to de-identified EEG, EOG, EMG, and sensor streams (excluding video) reduces privacy-compliance review and legal burden, enabling BIDS-compliant data sharing to OpenNeuro or similar repository within 12 months post-publication. Systemic: Open physiological dataset attracts independent research teams for algorithm replication and phenotyping reanalysis, amplifying publication impact and citation count by estimated 40–60% versus closed-cohort publication alone. Strategic: Secondary-use research partnerships reduce year-three algorithm development cost and strengthen phase-two grant competitiveness for DFG follow-up; bedroom video remains institutional-only, balancing scientific generalizability against participant privacy and avoiding legal entanglement.
options: Deposit complete de-identified EEG, EOG, EMG, mattress-sensor, microphone, and environmental-sensor streams under CC-BY-4.0 open license with minimalist consent constraints, enabling unrestricted reuse and third-party analysis but accepting privacy risk if re-identification via temporal fingerprinting becomes feasible.

Restrict data sharing to signed data use agreements (DUA) managed via institutional repository with access-request workflow, retaining institutional control over secondary uses and enabling researcher accountability but limiting discoverability and reducing third-party adoption by estimated 60–70%.

Deposit curated physiological subsets (standardized EEG montage, EMG, first-night and final-night PSG segments only) under open license while archiving full sensor logs and video internally under DUA, balancing openness with privacy by omitting continuous microphone and longest-term contact-free mattress data.
review: Controls **Scientific Openness vs. Privacy Protection**. Weakness: Option 1 assumes temporal de-identification is sufficient against re-identification via sleep-architecture clustering and site-specific environmental-sound acoustics, but no current standard definitively prevents re-identification from EEG time series combined with participant demographics—published literature shows risk remains non-negligible even under aggressive de-identification.

name: Environment Design Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Domestic-style décor and reduced clinical cues lower stress-arousal suppression → Systemic: Naturalistic parasomnia phenotypes emerge at higher frequency and fidelity, increasing 15–25% event capture per participant-night → Strategic: Publishable longitudinal morphology data becomes achievable within 24-month cohort window, reducing need for extended recruitment.
options: Preserve residential aesthetic with minimal safety modifications (padded edges hidden, standard bedroom furniture, soft lighting, no visible equipment racks); accept trade-off of slower emergency response time and higher facility insurance premium

Balance clinical safety with domestic feel through strategic placement (visible emergency call systems integrated into bedside tables, modular padding removable during day, standard hospital-grade fire systems behind residential finishes; target response time <90 seconds)

Optimize for maximum safety and regulatory clarity with semi-clinical design (visible safety rails, clinical-grade bed frames, institutional color scheme, 24/7 visible monitoring signage); accept likelihood of 20–30% behavioral suppression and slower event manifestation
review: Controls Naturalistic Behavior vs. Operational Safety. Weakness: The options do not address how different environments may systematically bias event morphology itself—e.g., whether a domestic setting triggers different seizure-like features than clinical settings, potentially confounding longitudinal comparisons across facility evolution.

name: Participant Selection Stringency
consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission polysomnography and collateral history screening identify higher-confidence parasomnia cases → Systemic: 75–85% event-yield rate achieved, reducing extension-stay burden and ethical exposure from extended unproductive admissions → Strategic: Faster publication timeline and lower attrition risk, but recruitment velocity drops 20–30%, potentially delaying month-18 enrollment target of 25 participants.
options: Require documented prior polysomnography with parasomnia event captured on record plus two independent physician assessments; enroll only participants meeting ICSD-3 diagnostic threshold with high certainty; expect 8–12 admissions per month at ~80% event yield but 15–20% overall referral rejection rate

Accept clinical interview and patient self-report of ≥2 episodes/month plus structured collateral history from bed partner; screen out only clear contraindications (active OSA, epilepsy risk); expect 15–20 admissions per month at ~65% event yield but faster recruitment ramp

Enroll broadly on self-reported parasomnia history alone with minimal pre-screening; rely on first-night structured PSG to confirm diagnosis; expect 25–30 admissions per month but only 45–55% event-yield rate, increasing unproductive-admission ceiling violations and ethical strain on participants
review: Controls Diagnostic Confidence vs. Recruitment Speed. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between participants with prodromal or sub-threshold parasomnia symptoms who may develop diagnosable events over extended monitoring—this population is clinically interesting but excluded by conservative criteria and inflates unproductive-admission rates under permissive criteria.

name: Sensing Acquisition Tiering
consequences: Immediate: Nightly dry-EEG headbands + contact-free mattress/infrared provide low-burden continuous monitoring with minimal privacy intrusion → Systemic: Scheduled enhanced PSG on days 1, mid-stay, final capture physiological state evolution at ~40–50% of full-montage cost → Strategic: Escalation nights triggered by technician flagging enable 90%+ clinical-grade capture of recurrent events, supporting automated triage benchmarking and 2–3 publications within budget.
options: Full PSG montage (scalp EEG 16+ channels, EOG, chin EMG, bilateral leg EMG, respiration, oxygen saturation, ECG) nightly for all participants; maximize physiological granularity and automated detection potential; consume €180K+ equipment budget and require 4 technicians per suite rotation; enable definitive state-transition analysis but exceed year-one spending ceiling

Nightly dry-electrode EEG headband (4 channels) + contact-free mattress pressure + ambient IR camera + microphone on all nights; enhanced full PSG on study nights 1, 7–14, final; escalation PSG within 24 hours of technician-flagged movement anomalies; balance cost (~€90K equipment), privacy, and clinical resolution; support triage tool development without full-montage overhead

Continuous actigraphy wristband plus environmental motion sensors and ambient audio only; add laboratory PSG confirmation one week post-discharge; minimize privacy risk and equipment cost (~€35K) but sacrifice real-time detection, inter-rater agreement scoring, and phase-two intervention readiness
review: Controls Cost + Privacy vs. Clinical Resolution. Weakness: The options assume technician annotation accuracy is stable across nights, but fatigue-driven detection bias over a 3-year program is never addressed; escalation logic depends on subjective real-time flagging, which may miss subtle morphologies on low-resolution data.

name: Staffing Scalability Model
consequences: Immediate: Current plan deploys 3 technicians for 8 suites (1:2.7 ratio) with night-shift rotation ensuring safety margin → Systemic: Expansion to 12 suites requires either 4.4 technicians (0.4 FTE hiring complexity) or relaxation of safety coverage ratio to 1:3 or 1:4 → Strategic: Deferred expansion decision at month 12 gates €200–300K year-two staffing costs; staffing inflexibility becomes critical path bottleneck if enrollment exceeds 35 participants in year two.
options: Hire 4 full technicians and 2 part-time backups at project launch; cross-train on PSG operation and AASM scoring; front-load payroll costs (~€600K year one) but enable rapid scaling to 12 suites by month 18 without recruitment disruption or safety compromise; accept higher early burn rate and severance risk if enrollment is slower than projected

Maintain 3 full technicians and hire 1 per-diem technician pool (4–5 individuals on on-call status) as enrollment demand materializes; scale staffing to observed participant flow; defer major hiring until month 9–12 enrollment trendline is clear; save ~€150K year one but risk 4–8 week recruitment delays if demand spikes unexpectedly

Outsource night-shift technician coverage to a clinical polysomnography services contractor for year one; retain core 2 in-house technicians for data pipeline and quality oversight; minimize fixed payroll exposure but lose institutional knowledge, on-site troubleshooting agility, and direct control over event annotation consistency
review: Controls Cost Flexibility vs. Operational Readiness. Weakness: None of the options quantifies the cognitive and annotation-error burden that accumulates when part-time or contractor technicians rotate through the facility, potentially degrading inter-rater agreement metrics and publication-ready data quality by month 18.

name: Publication and Partnership Governance
consequences: Immediate: Pharmaceutical co-funding with 30-day pre-publication review window requires manuscript circulation 4 weeks before journal submission → Systemic: 2–3 first-author publications planned for year two are delayed 3–6 months, pushing first methods-validation paper into year 2.5 if industry input triggers revisions → Strategic: Industry partnership accelerates equipment procurement and participant recruitment incentives (up to €150K value), but publication momentum slips, potentially affecting grant renewal and phase-two funding competitiveness.
options: Secure DFG and University of Bonn funding exclusively; retain zero external partnerships; full editorial control with 2–3 peer-reviewed publications submitted by month 24; maintain academic authorship independence and agile publication timeline; accept budget ceiling of €3.8M with no upside co-funding for equipment or participant compensation enhancements

Accept pharmaceutical partner funding (projected €400–600K) contingent on 30-day pre-publication review and named acknowledgment of industry support; retain full publication rights and author order; front-load equipment and participant compensation flexibility; navigate regulatory and reputational considerations of industry partnership in sleep neuroscience

Establish formal commercial licensing pathway for event-triage algorithm output; reserve patent applications for computational innovations; license methodology to clinical device company for later commercialization; monetize phase-two outcomes; accept delayed publication (6–12 month embargo) and loss of open-science positioning for competitive advantage
review: Controls Scientific Independence vs. Resource Acceleration. Weakness: The options do not address conflict-of-interest management if pharma partnership includes exclusivity clauses on future RBD biomarker discovery or recruitment prioritization, which could lock the academic team into undisclosed commercial constraints spanning the 3-year program window.
